source_paper_title: Improved Guarantees for the A Priori TSP

context:
Page 5, Section 1.2 (after Theorem 6); reiterated in Page 38, Section 9 (Discussion).

We conjecture that the master route ratio is exactly \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\).
title: Determine the exact master route ratio for a priori TSP with depot
background_latex:
Let \((V,c)\) be a finite metric space (triangle inequality, symmetry, and \(c(v,v)=0\)) whose points \(V\) are called customers. Each customer \(v\in V\) is active independently with probability \(p(v)\in(0,1]\). A distinguished customer \(d\in V\) is a depot with \(p(d)=1\).

An \emph{a priori TSP tour} is a cyclic ordering (TSP tour) \(T\) visiting all vertices in \(V\), chosen before seeing which customers are active. Given a realized active set \(A\subseteq V\), the realized tour cost is the length of the tour obtained by \emph{skipping} inactive vertices in the order prescribed by \(T\); denote this realized cost by \(c(T[A])\). The objective value of \(T\) is \(\mathbb{E}[c(T[A])]\) where the expectation is over the independent activations.

For a nonempty subset \(S\subseteq V\) with \(d\in S\), a \emph{master route solution} based on \(S\) consists of:
1) a TSP tour on \(S\) of length \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{tsp}}(S,c)\), and
2) for each \(v\in V\setminus S\), two parallel edges connecting \(v\) to a nearest point in \(S\), where \(c(v,S):=\min\{c(v,s): s\in S\}\).

Under the standard analysis for master route solutions, the expected cost of the master route solution based on \(S\) is
\[\mathrm{MR}(S) := \mathbb{E}\Big[\mathbf{1}_{\{|A|\ge 2\}}\Big(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{tsp}}(S,c) + 2\sum_{v\in A} c(v,S)\Big)\Big],\]
where \(A\) is the random active set, and the indicator accounts for the convention that if fewer than two customers are active then one may shortcut to cost \(0\).

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}\) denote the minimum possible expected cost \(\min_T \mathbb{E}[c(T[A])]\) over all a priori tours \(T\) for \((V,c,p)\).

The \emph{master route ratio} (with depot) is the worst-case ratio between the best master route solution and the optimal a priori tour, i.e.
\[\rho^* := \sup_{(V,c,p)\,\text{with depot }d} \ \frac{\min\{\mathrm{MR}(S): \varnothing\ne S\subseteq V\}}{\mathrm{OPT}},\]
with the convention \(0/0:=1\) when needed.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the exact value of the master route ratio \(\rho^*\), i.e., compute
\[\rho^* := \sup_{(V,c,p)\,\text{with depot }d} \ \frac{\min\{\mathrm{MR}(S): \varnothing\ne S\subseteq V\}}{\mathrm{OPT}}\]
for the a priori TSP with independent activations and a depot \(d\) satisfying \(p(d)=1\). In particular, decide whether
\[\rho^* = \frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}.\]
latex:

location_in_paper: Page 5, Section 1.2 (after Theorem 6); reiterated in Page 38, Section 9 (Discussion).
open_problem_quote: We conjecture that the master route ratio is exactly \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture is about a worst-case supremum; one can search over finite metric instances and activation probabilities to find ratios exceeding candidate values, and the objective \(\min_S \mathrm{MR}(S)/\mathrm{OPT}\) can be computed/approximated algorithmically for small \(|V|\) by enumerating tours and subsets.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate hard instances (metrics plus probability vectors) can be represented parametrically and scored by a computable fitness signal (estimated or exact ratio \(\min_S \mathrm{MR}(S)/\mathrm{OPT}\)). An evolutionary loop can explore constructions (e.g., clustered metrics, cycle-like metrics, group duplications) to try to push the ratio above or close to \(1/(1-e^{-1/2})\), potentially yielding new extremal examples or tighter numerical bounds.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The exact master route ratio \(\rho^*\) (with depot) for the a priori TSP remains unresolved. The current state of the art is due to Blauth, Neuwohner, Puhlmann & Vygen ("Improved guarantees for the A Priori TSP"), who formalize \(\rho^*\) as the worst-case gap between the best master route solution \(\min_{\varnothing\ne S\subseteq V}\mathrm{MR}(S)\) and the optimal a priori tour cost \(\mathrm{OPT}=\min_T\mathbb{E}[c(T[A])]\). They prove nearly matching bounds \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}<\rho^*<2.6\) (numerically \(<2.584\) via a dual-feasible solution to a carefully aggregated LP), and they conjecture the exact value \(\rho^*=\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\). Their lower bound is given by a clustered-metric construction where each of \(n\) groups contains \(m\) co-located customers with tiny activation probability \(1/(2m)\), so that the best master route chooses only the depot (expected cost \(n\)) while \(\mathrm{OPT}\) scales like the expected number of active groups \(\approx n(1-e^{-1/2})\).

Historically, the master-route framework originates in the constant-factor approximation of Shmoys & Talwar, whose analysis implies the coarse universal upper bound \(\rho^*\le 3\) (with depot, and via conditioning also without). The 2025 work improves this by reducing general instances to \(\varepsilon\)-normalized ones (uniform tiny activation outside the depot) and then encoding the metric along an optimal a priori tour via aggregated variables \(C_k\) (expected contribution of edges spanning \(k\) hops) satisfying triangle inequalities. The key technical advance is a finite LP (after bucketing and further interval aggregation) that upper-bounds the best master route cost over all subsets \(S\), enabling a numerical certificate below 2.6.

At present, the main gap is closing the interval \(\big(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}},2.6\big)\) and proving (or refuting) that the clustered lower-bound family is extremal. Promising directions suggested by the 2025 approach include: (i) sharpening the LP relaxation/aggregation to eliminate numerical slack and potentially converge to \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\); (ii) proving structural theorems that reduce worst cases to highly symmetric normalized instances (beyond what is already shown) and then solving the resulting limiting optimization problem analytically; and (iii) exploring whether low-activity regimes (small \(\sum_v p(v)\)) can ever yield a larger \(\rho^*\) than the high-activity clustered construction, a question explicitly left open in the discussion.

sources.title: Improved guarantees for the A Priori TSP
sources.bibtex:
@article{BlauthNeuwohnerPuhlmannVygen2025Improved,
  author  = {Blauth, Jannis and Neuwohner, Meike and Puhlmann, Luise and Vygen, Jens},
  title   = {Improved guarantees for the {A} Priori {TSP}},
  journal = {Mathematics of Operations Research},
  year    = {2025},
  volume  = {50},
  number  = {4},
  pages   = {2909--2940},
  doi     = {10.1287/moor.2023.0322},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Defines the master route ratio \(\rho^*\) (with depot) and proves \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}<\rho^*<2.6\). Provides a matching-style lower-bound construction approaching \(\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\) and an LP-based upper bound \(2.584\) (hence <2.6). Conjectures \(\rho^*=\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem of determining the exact master route ratio with depot and specifically whether \(\rho^*=\frac{1}{1-e^{-1/2}}\). It provides the current best known bounds and the conjectured exact value.

sources.title: A constant approximation algorithm for the a priori traveling salesman problem
sources.bibtex:
@inproceedings{ShmoysTalwar2008AprioriTSP,
  author    = {Shmoys, David and Talwar, Kunal},
  title     = {A constant approximation algorithm for the a priori traveling salesman problem},
  booktitle = {Integer Programming and Combinatorial Optimization (IPCO)},
  year      = {2008},
  pages     = {331--343},
  publisher = {Springer},
  doi       = {10.1007/978-3-540-68891-4_23},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the randomized sampling master-route framework for a priori TSP and proves a constant-factor approximation (4 randomized, 8 deterministic as stated in the source paper), with an analysis implying an upper bound \(\rho^*\le 3\) for the master route ratio (with depot and, via conditioning, without depot).
sources.connection: Provides the baseline master-route analysis that yields the classical upper bound \(\rho^*\le 3\), against which later work (including the 2025 paper) improves and which motivates determining the exact worst-case master route ratio.

sources.title: Deterministic sampling algorithms for network design
sources.bibtex:
@article{vanZuylen2011DeterministicSampling,
  author  = {van Zuylen, Anke},
  title   = {Deterministic sampling algorithms for network design},
  journal = {Algorithmica},
  year    = {2011},
  volume  = {60},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {110--151},
  doi     = {10.1007/s00453-009-9344-x},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops deterministic sampling/conditional-expectation techniques; as specialized/analyzed in the 2025 paper, yields a deterministic \((2+\alpha\rho)\)-approximation for a priori TSP with depot given master route ratio \(\rho\) and a TSP algorithm bounded relative to the subtour LP.
sources.connection: While not determining \(\rho^*\), it shows why tightening \(\rho^*\) matters: improved bounds on \(\rho^*\) translate directly into improved deterministic approximation guarantees for a priori TSP.

sources.title: A priori solution of a traveling salesman problem in which a random subset of the customers are visited
sources.bibtex:
@article{Jaillet1988AprioriTSP,
  author  = {Jaillet, Patrick},
  title   = {A priori solution of a traveling salesman problem in which a random subset of the customers are visited},
  journal = {Operations Research},
  year    = {1988},
  volume  = {36},
  number  = {6},
  pages   = {929--936},
  doi     = {10.1287/opre.36.6.929},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Early foundational formulation and analysis of the probabilistic/a priori TSP model with random active subsets, establishing the problem setting studied by later approximation work.
sources.connection: Gives original context for the a priori TSP objective \(\mathbb{E}[c(T[A])]\) and motivates master-route-type heuristics whose worst-case ratio \(\rho^*\) is being pinned down.

sources.title: A priori optimization
sources.bibtex:
@article{BertsimasJailletOdoni1990AprioriOptimization,
  author  = {Bertsimas, Dimitris J. and Jaillet, Patrick and Odoni, Amedeo R.},
  title   = {A priori optimization},
  journal = {Operations Research},
  year    = {1990},
  volume  = {38},
  number  = {6},
  pages   = {1019--1033},
  doi     = {10.1287/opre.38.6.1019},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies the broader framework of a priori optimization problems, including probabilistic routing problems, providing structural context for a priori TSP and related approximation approaches.
sources.connection: Places the master-route ratio question within the general a priori optimization paradigm and helps interpret \(\rho^*\) as a limitation of a widely used sampling-and-extend methodology.
